item.title: 2026-06 Summary
item.description:
Since I’ve been quite inconsistent, I will be switching to monthly summaries covering anything interesting in that month in no particular order.

IT

  • Google introduced DiffusionGemma as name suggest uses diffusion architecture instead of standard transformer approach. The advantage is faster token generation for the sake of model hallucinations1.
  • I finally watched The Thinking Game, a short movie about how Google DeepMind tackled the protein folding problem. It’s definitely worth a watch.
    • Watching it reminded me how my colleagues from the lab got excited and wanted to run it out on our GPU server. It took me few days to set it up correctly2.
  • Bloomberg discusses AI and safety with Dario and Daniela Amodei in Inside Anthropic, the $965 Billion AI Juggernaut.
  • How to Setup a Local Coding Agent on macOS by Kyle Howells compares token generation using gemma-4-26B-A4B (58.2 tokens/sec) vs adding MTP Draft mode (72.2 tokens/second). Interestingly, llama.cpp was clearly faster in all the tests compared to mlx which was a surprise.
  • Papers With Code is back and offers curated papers from arXiv and Hugging Face.
  • WWDC26 presented apple/container used to run linux containers. I am curious to see benchmarks comparing docker and podman runtime3.
  • Scripting good practices in Python by Bite code! provides some nice examples. Here are some of my favorites
    • Storing secrets using keyring library instead of relying on environment variables
    • argparse.RawDescriptionHelpFormatter for cli with no arguments
    • Custom exit function applied to sys.excepthook
  • Yolov5 is out and it’s impressive as usual, I suggest you try the iOS app and walk around your area.
    • I am also including a nice example by Marco Franzon where YOLO was finetuned to track immune cell chasing bacteria.
  • Fernando Irarrázaval was curious if people could break his openclaw instance (assistant Fiu) using prompt injection to leak the secrets thought an email summarized in What happened after 2,000 people tried to hack my AI assistant.
    • He chose Claude Opus 4.6 which already comes with strong guard rails. Interestingly, Fiu recognized over time that this was not an organic traffic and stored this info in its memory.
    • Although Fiu didn’t leak the secrets, was it worth the 500$ cost?

Science

  • A gene-therapy trial focusing on partial rejuvenation of older cells in eyes has been administered to first patient executed by Life Bioscience4 (co-founded by Prof. David A. Sinclair). Using inducible adenovirus the goal is to activate 3 reprogramming genes (OCT4, SOX2, KLF4 aka OSK genes from Yamanaka factors). The system only activates if antibiotics called doxycycline is administered56.
  • China deployed artificial human embryos (on batch cultured on uterine cells and second placed inside a microfluid chip) to Tiangong space station, while keeping control cells on Earth. The objective is to study the impact of space environment on the embryo development. The objective is to see if in future life can be started on other planets7.
  • Daraxonrasib8 a newly proposed drug prolongs the lifespan of patients suffering from advanced pancreatic cancer which suffer from RAS-mutations.
    • The study shows that the treatment was able to double the life expectation (however no patient survived to my understanding). It is still a promising new treatment with positive feedback from the community9.
  • A nice write-up by Eaton about Exploiting vulnerabilities in Johnson & Johnson web apps.
  • Cultrera di Montesano et al., 2026 focuses on improving cell type prediction by leveraging hierarchical ontologies. As cell type annotation is primarily manual, sometimes different granularity level are assigned to the cells (lv 0: lymphocytes, lv 1: B cells). This could be tackled by ontologies, where cell state would be assigned based on cumulative score of hierarchical representation of ontology rather than just a single-predicted probability score. In nutshell you use parent/children relationship when determine cell type, giving you higher confidence score #paper.
    • It is intuitive and elegant implementation termed hierarchical cross-entropy loss by the authors. The approach showcases moderate improvements in cell type prediction on quite a large datasets.
  • DenAdel et al., 2026 showcased that large number of cells ≠ better performance. The authors conclude that ~20,000 cells might be the sweet spot for training dataset. What does this mean for the future? Maybe we don’t need Billion cell project, but rather better understanding on how to sample enough cell diversity and choosing the right model architecture with optimized parameters #paper.
  • A short interview Why AI Hasn’t Cured Anything…Yet, According to Jennifer Doudna.
  • Merchant, Guo and Viggiano et al., 2026 introduce a generative programming language Proto for synthetic biology. The main objective is to provide infrastructure for designing DNA, RNA and protein sequences with defined generative and optimizer modules. Basically a constrained generative system for quick assembly. As the authors point out in the discussion, the main challenge was to standardize multiple tools required for this project. Overall, I can imagine Proto being utilized by both wet and dry-labs combined with AI to facilitate faster discovery, removing the installation/infrastructure barrier shifting the focus and energy to research instead of figuring out why tools are not working on HPC. Lastly, I appreciate the disclosure transparency of AI usage for developing and writing the manuscript.
  • QED Science, which estimates quality score for scientific papers took X/Twitter by storm with interesting debates and commentaries10.

  1. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Dxn3BcSgsMY ↩︎

item.title: Precision medicine
item.description:
Some articles/website on applied precision medicine

  • Sid Sijbrandij’s treatment of osteosarcoma in the T5 vertebrae of the upper spine1
  • From flue signs all the way to encephalitis, an autoimmune disease2
  • Baby BBC chromosome

  1. https://sytse.com/cancer/ ↩︎

  2. https://burntsushi.net/encephalitis/ ↩︎

item.title: Just Fucking Use Go
item.description: An appealing article by Blain Smith on about why using Go. I don’t particularly care for the grotesque language, but I like some of the arguments. Having a language which is designed for performance, comes with dev tool sets and can be compiled? Of course there are other languages which can do the same and don’t suffer from the endless nil validation, but this was a design choice on purpose.

item.title: Untitled
item.description: From the creator of Obsidian (Steph Ango), convert any webpage to markdown using Defuddle. At the moment, 1,000 pages per month are free using API. Works quite well even on research publications and is open-sourced at kepano/defuddle.

item.title: Untitled
item.description:
This is a list of git tricks I’ve found randomly on the Internet that could be helpful.

Amend any commit hash with 

# .gitignore
[alias]
 fixup = "!f() { TARGET=$(git rev-parse $1); git commit --fixup=$TARGET ${@:2} && GIT_SEQUENCE_EDITOR=true git rebase -i --autostash --autosquash $TARGET^; }; f"

running git fixup <SHA-1>.

item.title: Untitled
item.description:
There is a new Parque parser codenamed Hardwood which has been build from scratch and allows faster parsing with less dependencies. At the moment it’s available for Java only, but there is a cli tool which can be utilized right away.

Link to article here.

item.title: ImGajeed76/pgit
item.description: The tool written by Oliver Seifert pgit can load git history to a Postgres database in order to perform further analysis. Pgit was build as a demo which uses a compression library xpatch under the hood.

item.title: RustFS
item.description: MinIO a popular s3 compatible storage made some headlines past few month where they removed UI interface from the community license and then later switched repository to maintenance mode. This move forced FOOS community to create a fork pgsty/minio while others are now switching to RustFS.

item.title: Pachter et al., 2026 bioRxiv
item.description: The work of Pachter et al., focuses on porting well established edgeR package to Python (edgePython) using Claude Opus 4.5, 4.6 and Codex. As described in the paper, the edgeR is a non trivial package supporting various functions used for differential expression analysis in genomics research. Due to Python’s popularity in data science, having edgeR natively available in Python ecosystem, would be highly beneficial for the scientific community. Although, there exists alternative solutions of combining/converting R and Python objects interchangeably,1 they can be cumbersome.

item.title: The missing semester from MIT 2026
item.description: The famous IAP 2026 course which teaches the fundamentals not covered in classic CS classes has been finally updated and released. I always like these types of courses as they are nice refresher for some fundamentals, but manage to always surprise me with some new tricks.

item.title: AlexsJones/llmfit
item.description:
llmfit written by Alex Jones can help you figure out which LLM model best fits your gear setup. Versatile tools with many settings.

Written in Rut, can be installed with cargo or brew

brew tap AlexsJones/llmfit
brew install llmfit

and then simply running

item.title: Integrating Jan with opencode
item.description: This weekend I went back to test again Jan.ai for running LLM models locally on my MacBook. I do like the simple interface and already build in web-search using exa. From my crude testing I concluded MLX optimized models run faster compared to llama.cpp and I wanted to test it further with opencode. Since I haven’t found official documentation on how to integrate mlx-lm with opencode I’m providing links and settings on how to achieve it.

item.title: Integrating Jan.ai with opencode
item.description: This weekend I went back to test again Jan.ai for running LLM models locally on my MacBook. I do like the simple interface and already build in web-search using exa. From my crude testing I concluded MLX optimized models run faster compared to llama.cpp and I wanted to test it further with opencode. Since I haven’t found official documentation on how to integrate mlx-lm with opencode I’m providing links and settings on how to achieve it.

item.title: Text classification with zstd
item.description: Max Halford showcases how Zstandard compression (zstd) library from Facebook can be used for text classification. This is achieved because the zstd supports incremental compression. The trick Max shows is that one can build multiple topic classifiers and the one returning the smallest size when using test set, is the right one. The module (compression.zstd) was introduced in Python 3.14. There is an interesting discussion on HN about limitations of this approach for AI.

item.title: LLM Visualization
item.description: Brendan Bycroft created an online app which visually walks you through architecture and steps of LLM models (GPT-2 small, nano-gpt, GT2 (XL) or GPT-3).

item.title: Trying to run local LLM on linux server
item.description:
I’ve been testing a local LLM on my trusty MacBook Pro M4 16GB using ollama. However, this configuration is limited to only smaller models with quantization usually around 4 bits. Luckily enough, I have access to a linux server with some GPUs to play with. When it comes to running LLM locally with CUDA, based on my reading the options are either ollama, vLLM or llama.cpp.

I wanted to try something new so I started first with vllm and boy oh boy, installing this was a nightmare. The latest wheels for vLLM (v0.15.1) are compiled for CUDA 13 (I have only access to CUDA 12 on the HPC). I started first with recommended approach using uv and --torch-backend=auto. After a while I am hitting the first barrier also described in issue #7785.

item.title: Epsilon in AdamW matters
item.description:
When training LLM, Sifal shows that switching from default eps=1e-8 to eps=1e-10 in AdamW optimizer can lead to better results. He showcases this on his toy example where default epsilon oscillates when searching for local minima compared to proposed one. However, this only applies when training is NOT done with half-precision.

Klioui, S. (2026). The Epsilon Trap: When Adam Stops Being Adam. Sifal Klioui Blog. link

item.title: microsoft/BitNet
item.description: A new paradigm shift from Microsoft called BitNet, where the objective is to reduce speed and footprint of LLM models, by storing the LLM weights as 1bit. This is done during training, compared to quantization which fixes the weights post training. Authors report promising benchmarking with BitNet b1.58 2B using only 400MB RAM and 29ms latency. It will be interesting to see where we will see these types of models deployed in future. Targets could be phones, IoT or privacy focused closed offline systems. For more in-depth details, I highly recommend watching this great explanation by Julia Turc.

item.title: Osteoarthritis could be treated in future with drug inhibitor
item.description: A new research from Stanford showed promising results tackling osteoarthritis (degenerative joint disease) by inhibition of 15-hydroxy prostaglandin dehydrogenase (15-PGDH). They categorize this gene as gerozyme, meaning it increases its activity with age. The research shows that inhibition (down-regulation of signal) helps with rejuvenation of the cartilage. The hope is that in future, simple drug treatment could help with restoration of cartilage targeting elderly population, post-surgery patients or ACL-like injuries. This could be huge for osteoarthritis since cartilage rejuvenation is primarily done with stem cells. So far phase 1 clinical trials confirmed safety of drug administration in mice.

item.title: Time to play with LG TV
item.description: If you own a LG TV and are completely fed up with the random endless impossible to skip Youtube Ads, you have two options. Either buy the subscription, or install Youtube AdFree via WebOSBrew. To do that you have to decide between rooting your TV (voids the warranty) or enable development mode which has it’s limitations but can be a good starting point. If interested, follow the guide here. Kudos to the developers and the amazing work which allows developing and installing apps outside of LG’s operating system ecosystem.

item.title: Video transcoding
item.description:
Thought out my new year cleanup, I discovered that some of my movies in (mkv) are not discovered by Jellyfin. I’m still not quite sure what might be the cause, but it was perfect timing because I wanted to look more into ripping some movies with ffmpeg. There are plethora settings, codecs, encoders to choose from, just have a look at reddit r/ffmpeg. Below is my good enough info for now

  1. Use H.265 encoder, it’s newer, generates smaller size than H.264
  2. For sounds use it’s recommended to use AV1

or if you don’t want to deal with this, just use HandBrake which already has well predefined settings. Note for Mac users, use hardware acceleration settings in Presents -> Hardware -> H.265 (either 4k or 1080p).

item.title: Migrating Google Photos to Immich
item.description:
TL;DR: This is a summary post of my experience migrating Google Photos to immich. Did you setup your reverse-proxy correctly? Spoiler alert, I didn’t 🤦‍♂️

  1. Navigate to Google Takeout and request all your Google Photos images. I suggest to use tar compression.
  2. Download fantastic tool immich-go, which will import all photos including metadata, albums etc.
  3. Generate an API KEY with specified permissions. For simplicity I’ve generated a key with all permissions which I’ve immediately deleted after import was finished. 
    # Import command from documentation
./immich-go upload from-google-photos \
	--server=http://10.0.0.103:2283 \ # only use hostname if you've correctly configured reverse-proxy
	--api-key=<API_KEY> \
	~/Downloads/Takeout/Google\ Photos/
  4. After successful import I’ve used shtse8/Google-Photos-Delete-Tool to delete all Google Photos, because the UI doesn’t support selecting all photos in one go. I’ve simply pasted the JS code in console and waited until all images were automatically selected and then clicked delete button.

Troubleshooting random import crashes

If like me (and many others), you haven’t properly configured your reverse-proxy, you probably experienced a random crash during importing. My initial thoughts were performance issue as described here, however the error message was hinting on something else

item.title: Week 26 (23-29/06/2025)
item.description:
👾 IT

  • AI talk by Andrej Karpathy which took internet by storm link.
  • Although not the most secure solution for DNS (i.e. TLS), but still nicely visualized link.
  • Youtube’s fake buffering and its breakdown link.
  • Another massive #password leak link.
    • As always 2FA + password manager can help reducing the risk
  • Another great write-up by Cloudflare about blocking large DDoS attack link.
  • If you MacBook gives you buzzing sensation, you are not alone link!
    • Apple time to fix this issue …

🔬Science

  • Scientists identity all kinds of information by collecting just environment DNA in Dublin.link.
    • Could this be done on a large scale to track uprising diseases?
  • Promising tooth implants tested in rats link.
  • Liu’s lab created specialized electrodes from fluorinated elastomers which can be attached and “expand” with developing brain in order to measure neuron activity link.

🔗 Links

  • Finland introduces sand battery to store heat as a cost-efficient and sustainable alternative link.
  • Timeline of Virtual Cells concept link.
  • DuckLake: Mix of metadata and files link.

🔧 Tools

  • EbodShojaei/bake: Make formatter and linter
  • axboe/fio: Benchmarking tool for I/O

item.title: Week 24 (09-15/06/2025)
item.description:
👾 IT

  • Commentary from Garry Marcus on the latest criticism of LLMs “reasoning” from Apple (link).
    • Interesting notes:
      • LLM might fail from inference outside of training distribution (out-of-distribution), same problem as perceptron
      • The main take home is that LLMs are not thinking, at least based on puzzle examples in the paper
  • OS for public sector by EU, currently POC (link).

🔬Science

  • Focused ultrasound stimulation could be the alternative treatment in future (link).
    • Idea started with chronic inflammation; possible other candidates are diabetes (already tested on mice, pigs and was published)
    • Could be converted into wearable device in future, allowing easy access for anyone

🔗 Links

  • Marco writes about his own experience with Severely Deficient Autobiographical Memory / Aphantasia (link)
    • TL;DR: Difficulty to recall information through visualization
    • Youtube: Missing Mind’s Eye: Solving the Mystery of Aphantasia
  • DIY budget server for AI/ML (link)
  • Emerging Startup AheadComputing focuses on building the next new CPU (link)
  • Apple really likes to make those API calls (link).

🔧 Tools

  • resemble-ai/chatterbox: Resemble AI’s first production-grade open source TTS model.
  • AasishPokhrel/shit: This is kinda funny 😅.

item.title: Week 23 (02-08/06/2025)
item.description:
👾 IT

  • This is why data structure matters, hashmap to reduce tracking of duplicates decreased backup runtime. Fix was submitted to upsteram git repo (link).
  • Analysis of Cloudflare’s Claude-Generated code documented by the team via commit history (link).
  • Machine Learning Mastery back with some useful 10 one-liners in python (link).

🔬Science

  • Likely not surprising but race and ethnicity do align so well with genetic background (link).
  • Bell Labs was all about science and innovation (link).
    • “How do you manage genius? You don’t.” And it worked. - Mervin Kelly
  • Fibers on the surface of cotton seeds dissolved in lithium bromide-water and heat up to high temperature creates transparent material similartly strong to a polycarbonate. It’s biodegradable and can reduce half of CO2 emissions compared to plastic (link).
  • Titanium dioxide which is in food, was shown to dysregulate endocrine system in mice. Although banned in Europe, America still watis for government’s response to the current research (link).

🔗 Links

  • Great to Simon’s journey to self-hosting (link).
  • Let’s Encrypt tackles zombie clients with pausing/unpausing approach (link).
  • Did you know you can store your data on paper with the right conversion (link)?
  • MonsterUI packs FastHTML with Tailwind to generate quickly web-apps with Python (link).

🔧 Tools

  • iamgio/quarkdown: another markdown with superpowers
  • onlook-dev/onlook: the Cursor but for designers in mind
  • elebumm/RedditVideoMakerBot: automated reddit videos
  • wandb/wandb: AI developer platform

item.title: Week 22 (25-30/05/2025)
item.description:
👾 IT

  • Simon’s initial impression of Devstal from Mistral (link).
  • Namanyay provides very simple yet convincing argument about how to leverage the AI landscape in your day-to-day dev life. It’s not perfect, but it can be simplify some tasks, you just need to figure out which ones (link).
  • Cloudflare proposes HTTP message signature to simplify how to deal with ai bots/crawlers (link).
  • Oddysey introduced model which generates AI video with real-time interaction from user (link).
  • MinIO moves many features behind paywall minio/object-browser.
    • Alternative switch: Garage.
  • Nice tips & tricks on running LLM locally by Kiran (link).

🔬Science

  • FDA pushes initiative to replace animal models for pre-clinical studies by embracing organ-on-a chip or in silico modeling if it offers to be a viable alternative(link).
  • More bones found in the “River of Death” in Alberta, Canada provides great resource to study Pachyrhinosaurus and other dinosaurs (link).
  • New #podcast series A Coffee with CompBio (link)
  • Zeng et al., 2025 develops CellFM model using ERetNet paired with LoRA.
  • Tackling how to extract number of sequenced cells from papers using LLM by Svensson (link).
  • Sikkema et al., 2025 BiorXiv developed MapQC to estimate fitness of mapping between reference and query
    • Energy distance metric (E-distance) measures shift between two distributions (signal-to-noise ratio)

🔗 Links

  • Internet Archive launches Youtube channel where they stream how they scan material using high-resolution camera, use automated software for cropping and OCR conversion and archive it afterward on microfiche cards (link).
  • Faster Github Actiosn from Blacksmith company with their transparency on security layers (link).
  • Once provide a phone number it will find its way to the interwebs (link).

🔧 Tools

  • simonw/llm: LLM from terminal simply.
  • awslabs/mountpoint-s3: Mount s3 bucket which is POSIX compliant.
  • antinomyhq/forge: AI assistant directly in terminal.
  • facebookincubator/cinder: Meta’s version of CPython focused on speed performance.

item.title: Week 21 (19-24/05/2025)
item.description:
👾 IT

  • Well, Google just dropped Veo 3 at I/o Keynote which generates quite realistic shorts videos (link).

🔬Science

  • Long read on about mitochondria, their fascinating history (originally bacteria which was consumed by an ancestral cell long time ago); fascinating functioning thought “social” queues and link to numerous diseases (link).
  • Gharibi et al., 2025 introduces 3D model culturing hESCs towards post-gastrulation amnion. A big milestone as it allows researchers to study and perturb this developmental process in vitro. These models could be provided as an alternative amniotic membranes in future clinical settings (link).
  • Abukar et al., 2025 uses light-sheet microscopy to track hearth development in mouse providing new insights into the intricacies of cell movement and coordination (link).
  • Great podcast episode from Quanta Magazine discussing latest research on how LLM are not as great as initially thought when it comes to learning “new” languages (link).

🔗 Links

  • Using specialized process to treatment steel with stainless steel, a young startup Allium Engineering can extend the lifetime of steel by making it resistant against corrosion (link).
  • Have I Been Pwned 2.0 is out packing some new features (link).
  • The Semantic Search Engine for ArXiv (link).
  • When you live around Bay Area and you want to save some money on electricity with solar, have a look at this deep dive by Joe (link).

🔧 Tools

  • langgenius/dify: interface for building LLM apps
  • kortix-ai/suna: open-source generalist #ai-agent
  • jlowin/fastmcp: build MPC fast with Python
  • kamranahmedse/developer-roadmap: yet another developer roadmap
  • mlabonne/llm-course: LLM #course with code
  • deepspeedai/DeepSpeed: library for optimized training
  • apple/ml-fastvlm: run visual encoding on iPhones from #apple
  • bytedance/deer-flow: deep-research framework packed with extra integrations
  • Lightricks/LTX-Video: impressive video generator

item.title: Daily from 2025-05-12
item.description:
👾 IT

  • Awesome 14 Advanced Python Features summarized by Edward (link)

🔬Science

  • Quite an old new but did you know that limpet teeth are apparently worlds strongest biological material (only 1mm in legth!)? (link)

🔗 Links

  • Nehal writes about how few books changed his view on biology and sparked a new exciting journey towards better understanding of life’s mystery (link)
  • A long read about Jack Tramiel and history of Commodore, Atari and his resilience towards “Computers for the masses, not the classes”. (link)

🔧 Tools

  • Have you listened yet to your GitHub contribution graph? (link)

item.title: Daily from 2025-05-05
item.description:
👾 IT

  • MCP for agents to “talk” with your resume? Is this where the job market will move one day? (link)
  • Imagine coding without a screen using AR glasses and peripherals. Although portable, still quite pricey for my taste (link)

🔗 Links

  • Nice and clean default settings for macOS (link). Thank you Catalin’s Tech!
# Enable tap-to-click for the trackpad
defaults write com.apple.AppleMultitouchTrackpad Clicking -bool true
defaults -currentHost write -g com.apple.mouse.tapBehavior -int 1
# Disable the .DS file creation
defaults write com.apple.desktopservices DSDontWriteNetworkStores -bool true
defaults write com.apple.desktopservices DSDontWriteUSBStores -bool true

item.title: Daily from 2025-05-01
item.description:
👾 IT

  • It’s happening, pylock.toml is supported by pip and uv and are we experiencing a fight for type checkers? (link)
  • Alex showcases how efficiently export and import sqlite #database - awesome hack! (link)
    • $ sqlite3 explorer.db .dump | gzip -c > explorer.db.txt.gz
  • Ibrahim has enough of bots and fights them with a clever zip bomb (link)

🔬Science

  • Satirical life journey of a PhD student (link). I wonder how many PhDs can relate to this story 🤔

🔗 Links

  • Thanks to metagenomics, doctors cured Bristol’s eyesight infection by targeted antibiotics treatment. This showcases we need access to precision medicine (link)
  • FDA approves genetically modified pigs using CRISPR which can be safely eaten. The genetic modification boosts immune system of pigs agains 99% of known PRRS viruses. Would you still eat them knowing this? (link)

🔧 Tools

  • With dataframely you can specify a validation schema for your polars (link)

item.title: Daily from 2025-04-24
item.description:
👾 IT

  • Easy to follow granular explanation of how DeepSeek was trained by Anita from vellum (link)

🔗 Links

  • Another reminder to keep thinking about #privacy (link)
  • Looks like gladiators really fought wild animal based on some interesting discoveries in York (link)
  • Interesting #history lesson on post-war effect of Agent Orange during Vietnam war. The side effects of the agent are associated to developmental disabilities still today (link)
  • tim shows how he used mitmproxy to track the trackers (link)

item.title: Daily from 2025-04-23
item.description:
👾 IT

  • Interesting take on Model Context Protocol (MCP); it’s not about the data, it’s about how the chatbot will integrate with it (link)

🔬Science

  • #history Margaret Dayhoff who laid foundations for the field of bioinformatics. (link)
  • CT scans can increase a risk of cancer and therefore should be justified before applying them on patients, but beware of limitations of the study. (link)

🔗 Links

  • SeaCURE tries to remove CO2 from the water to reduce climate change. They do so by converting the sea water more acidic which releases CO2 which is further stored. (link)
  • Latest measurements of toothpaste content reveal high levels of toxic metals way above FDA standard limits. (link)
  • By donating (transplanting) a womb to her younger sister Grace, Grace was able to give birth to a child through in-vitro fertilization (IVF). The womb transplantation was first performed in Sweden in 2014. (link)
  • Personalized humanoids under review (link)

item.title: Daily from 2025-04-21
item.description:
👾 IT

  • Work from Martin Karsten where he focuses on optimizing network processing in Linux which can reduce up to 30% CPU consumption, making this interesting use case for data centers. However best results are only possible for high-traffic scenarios (link).
  • Philip Laine writes about his experience with Microsoft forking his Spegel project as Peerd (link)
  • #python PEP 750 accepts t-string (https://davepeck.org/2025/04/11/pythons-new-t-strings/)

🔬Science

  • Scientists show in mice that IL-17 cytokine plays two divergent roles - in amygdala it triggers anxiety but in cortex it promotes social behaviour. The study highlights possible link between immune system and nervous system suggesting possibilities new therapies for neural disorders by affecting modulating immune system (link).

🔗 Links

  • brainstorm.gg is a website for brainstorming some fun ideas

🔧 Tools

  • nari-labs/dia is a 1.6B model capable of generating ultra-realistic dialogue with open-weights published on Hugging Face

Projects

This is a short list of projects I’ve been working on over the past few years.

PhD

Thesis: From high dimensionality to developmental biology - understanding the evolution of cell identity in the early embryo

  • ccmnet: Detection of causal interactions from scRNA-seq datasets
  • Preimplantation DL models: DL models for predicting cell types in early mouse and human development (web portal, datasets, models)
  • nf-core/marsseq: Preprocessing pipeline for single-cell MARS-seq experiments
  • cat-python: Estimates similarity between clusters from different single-cell sequencing techniques

Small tools and utilities are available at github.com/brickmanlab

item.title: About
item.description:
Senior Bioinformatician / Computational Biologist

I am interested in developmental biology and precision medicine. I like to build and contribute to tools in the field of bioinformatics.
